title: The Sunk Cost Fallacy: Why You Finish Bad Movies and Stay in Wrong Relationships
description: Rational economics says only future benefits and costs should drive decisions. Your brain says otherwise. The sunk cost fallacy has bankrupted companies, prolonged wars, and trapped people in lives they hate.

segments.voice: Imagine you've paid 20 dollars for a movie ticket. Thirty minutes in, you realize the movie is terrible. You are bored, irritated, and certain it won't improve. What do you do? If you're like most people, you stay. Because you paid 20 dollars, and leaving feels like wasting that money. Now here's the rational question: the 20 dollars is gone regardless. Whether you leave or stay, you don't get it back. The only relevant question is: would you rather spend the next 90 minutes watching a bad movie, or doing something else? The 20 dollars is irrelevant to that calculation. But your brain refuses to accept that. This refusal is the sunk cost fallacy.
segments.visual: movie theater bad film stay leave ticket waste

segments.voice: The sunk cost fallacy is the tendency to continue an endeavor because of past investments — time, money, effort, or emotion — that cannot be recovered. The past investment becomes a psychological anchor that distorts evaluation of present and future options. Rational decision theory, formalized by economists, states clearly that sunk costs are irrelevant to future decisions. Only prospective costs and benefits should matter. The brain, however, does not make decisions by rational economic calculation. It makes decisions by loss aversion — and abandoning a prior investment feels like an additional loss.
segments.visual: sunk cost past investment loss aversion psychology

segments.voice: The neurological basis of the sunk cost fallacy involves the anterior cingulate cortex and the limbic system — regions associated with loss processing and emotional regulation. Research by Kuo-Wei Chen and colleagues using fMRI found that sunk cost sensitivity was associated with activation in the medial prefrontal cortex when participants were deciding whether to continue failing projects. The brain encodes the potential abandonment of a prior investment as a loss — not as a neutral reallocation of future resources. The pain of loss activates, and the decision is made to avoid that pain by continuing.
segments.visual: brain fMRI anterior cingulate loss processing sunk cost

segments.voice: The Concorde fallacy is the most famous institutional example of sunk costs driving catastrophic decisions. The Anglo-French governments that funded the Concorde supersonic passenger aircraft continued pouring billions of pounds and francs into the project long after internal analyses showed it would never be commercially viable. Both governments knew it. Engineers knew it. Economic advisors knew it. The project continued because so much had already been invested that stopping felt like admitting the prior expenditure was wasted. The project finally ended in 2003 after decades of losses. The fallacy is now named after the plane.
segments.visual: Concorde aircraft supersonic government funding continued loss

segments.voice: Military conflicts provide some of the deadliest sunk cost traps in history. The Vietnam War's escalation from 1965 to 1973, analyzed by historian Arthur Schlesinger and later by decision researcher Hal Arkes, shows a classic sunk cost pattern: each year, the prior investment in American lives and money made withdrawal feel like betraying those who had already died. President Nixon described the logic explicitly in private recordings: 'We cannot lose after all these sacrifices.' The future cost of continued escalation was evaluated through the lens of the past investment. The result was years of additional casualties to avoid admitting the original decision was wrong.
segments.visual: Vietnam War escalation military sacrifice sunk cost decision

segments.voice: Research on the sunk cost fallacy in business is extensive. Hal Arkes and Catherine Blumer, who coined the term in a 1985 paper, ran experiments in which participants were given a business scenario: their company had invested significant resources in a failing project, and a competitor had just launched a clearly superior alternative. Most participants recommended continuing the internal project because of the prior investment, even when continuing was economically dominated. The sunk cost effect was stronger when the prior investment was larger. More investment meant stronger commitment to the failing path.
segments.visual: business failing project sunk cost Arkes Blumer experiment

segments.voice: Software development is chronically afflicted by sunk cost fallacy. When a development team has spent months or years building a system that is clearly not working — technically flawed, architecturally wrong, or no longer relevant to user needs — the accumulated investment becomes a powerful force against the rational choice to start fresh. The technical term is legacy system lock-in, but the psychological mechanism is pure sunk cost. Many enterprises run critical infrastructure on systems that would never be chosen if built today, because the investment in the existing system creates too much pain to abandon.
segments.visual: software legacy system sunk cost technical debt rewrite

segments.voice: Relationships are perhaps the domain where the sunk cost fallacy causes the most personal suffering. Research on relationship persistence by Frank and Gilovich found that the duration of a relationship is a stronger predictor of continued commitment than current relationship quality. People stay in relationships that are manifestly unhealthy or unfulfilling not because they expect improvement, but because of the years already invested. 'We've been together eight years' is not a rational reason to continue. It is a sunk cost. The eight years are gone. The only relevant question is: what are the next years likely to look like?
segments.visual: relationship sunk cost years invested commitment unhappy

segments.voice: Educational and career paths are similarly distorted by sunk costs. Medical students who realize midway through their training that medicine is not what they want still complete the degree, not because the prospect of practicing medicine is appealing but because of the years already spent. Research by Bragger and colleagues found that when students were told they had completed 75 percent of a program, they were significantly more likely to report willingness to continue even when the program was described as going poorly. The completion percentage — a retrospective datum — was driving a prospective decision.
segments.visual: medical student career path sunk cost degree training

segments.voice: In gambling, sunk cost fallacy manifests as 'chasing losses' — a well-documented pattern in which gamblers who have lost money continue playing with the explicit goal of winning back what they lost. The lost money is sunk. The rational calculation says stop. The brain says: I'm down, so I need to keep going to recover. Research by Rachlin on gambling behavior shows that the size of prior losses is a stronger predictor of continued gambling than the current probability of winning. Casinos' design accounts for this: they provide seamless continuation from loss to next bet, and they make walking away feel like final confirmation of failure.
segments.visual: gambling chasing losses casino poker sunk cost fallacy

segments.voice: Diets and fitness programs are disrupted by sunk costs in an interesting reverse direction. Research by Brownell and colleagues on dietary adherence found that people who had previously failed to maintain a diet were more likely to abandon a new diet attempt early — the sunk cost of previous failed attempts created a 'what's the point' response that undermined new effort. Here the sunk cost is in the negative direction: past investment in failed attempts creates pessimism about future attempts. The sunk cost fallacy works in both directions — it can trap you in continuing or trap you in not starting.
segments.visual: diet failure sunk cost pessimism new attempt fitness

segments.voice: Confirmation bias and sunk cost fallacy amplify each other powerfully. When you have invested in a decision, you are motivated to believe it was correct. Confirmation bias then filters your information environment to support that belief. New evidence that the decision was wrong is discounted. The sunk cost provides the motivation; confirmation bias provides the mechanism. Together they create a belief-protection system that can sustain demonstrably wrong decisions for years. The investor who is most committed to a stock will be the last to hear the case for selling it.
segments.visual: confirmation bias sunk cost feedback loop investment stock

segments.voice: Hal Arkes proposed that the sunk cost fallacy may have evolved as an adaptive heuristic in ancestral environments. In a world with limited resources and no financial system, sunk investments in skills, tools, and social relationships were often genuinely indicative of future value — the effort that went into a tool or skill was correlated with its utility. Abandoning things that represented significant prior investment was, in that context, often a mistake. The heuristic was useful then. In a world of complex financial decisions, multi-year institutional projects, and personal relationships, the same heuristic misfires systematically.
segments.visual: evolutionary psychology ancestral heuristic adaptive sunk cost

segments.voice: The antidote to sunk cost fallacy, repeated across the research literature, is prospective framing: consciously reframing decisions as if you were starting fresh. 'Forget everything you've already invested. Given what you know right now, would you start this project, this relationship, this career? If you were hiring someone for this company today, would you hire the person currently in the role?' These questions bypass the emotional weight of prior investment and force evaluation of future prospects. They are cognitively effortful. They require deliberately suppressing a strong intuitive pull. But research shows they consistently improve decision quality.
segments.visual: prospective framing fresh start decision reset reframe

segments.voice: Project milestone reviews structured around the prospective question — rather than reviews of progress and investment — have been shown to improve corporate decision-making quality. Research by Kahneman and Lovallo at McKinsey found that companies that conducted 'premortem' analysis and 'outside view' assessments — explicitly excluding prior investment from their decision calculus — made significantly better investment continuation decisions. The structural intervention has to come from outside the team most invested in the project, because that team is neurologically the least capable of ignoring the sunk cost.
segments.visual: project review outside view premortem corporate decision

segments.voice: Loss aversion, the asymmetric psychological weight of losses versus equivalent gains, is the deepest reason sunk costs are so hard to ignore. Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory established that losses feel psychologically approximately twice as powerful as equivalent gains. Accepting that an investment is lost means experiencing that loss fully. Continuing the investment defers and potentially avoids the full acknowledgment of loss. The brain is optimizing for loss avoidance, not for future wellbeing. Finishing the bad movie is loss avoidance. Staying in the wrong relationship is loss avoidance. The math of your future life is irrelevant to the pain of admitting the past was wrong.
segments.visual: loss aversion prospect theory Kahneman Tversky psychology

segments.voice: The movie is almost over now. You've stayed through the bad parts. The investment you've made watching this video is sunk — you don't get those minutes back whether you finish or not. But here is the question prospective framing asks: given what you've learned, are the next minutes worth spending this way? The answer, with the research on sunk costs firmly in mind, should be clearer than it was before. The past cost is gone. The only thing that exists is the decision ahead of you, evaluated entirely on its own terms. The fallacy, once named and felt in your own thinking, becomes slightly harder to run without your noticing.
segments.visual: awareness decision future choice sunk cost resolution
